headlines.title: Police activity shuts down eastbound I-580 in Livermore
headlines.description: Police activity had shut down both directions of Interstate 580, east of Isabel Avenue, in Livermore, according to the California Highway Patrol. CHP Dublin on Friday afternoon reported just before 3 p.m. both directions of the freeway were still shut down. Just before 3:30 p.m., CHP announced only westbound lanes were opened. Just before 4 p.m. CHP said all lanes were reopened. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Deadly shooting near scene of Santa Clara house fire
headlines.description: Santa Clara police on Friday were searching a neighborhood for a suspect who shot and killed a man before possibly setting a house on fire. According to initial police communications, someone reported hearing about eight or nine gunshots at about 12:30 p.m. in the area of Serra and Enright avenues. Officers were heard saying they believed a man was walking to his car, possibly from a house, when another man shot him multiple times. Shortly thereafter, the house went up in flames. Firefighters used ladder trucks to douse the burning structure from above. Police confirmed the victim later died at the scene. Neighbors who said they knew the victim were horrified. “It’s horrible, it’s horrible,” Virginia Rogers said. “My neighbor’s husband got killed. They’re a very close family. It’s very, very sad. For her to find out, come home, and find your husband dead on the lawn, it’s horrible.” A resident who owns the house directly behind the burning home called the scene hectic. “I could see the smoke over my roof line,” the resident said. “The police had pulled up and had weapons drawn and ordered my wife and my daughter to evacuate the house, which they did pretty, pretty quickly.” The resident said his wife heard multiple gunshots. As of late Friday afternoon, police were searching homes in the area for the shooter. They plan to enter the house that caught fire to conduct a search there. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: San Francisco man convicted in fraudulent auto insurance scheme
headlines.description: A federal jury has convicted a San Francisco man of conspiracy, wire fraud, and money laundering in connection with a scheme involving fraudulent insurance claims on already-wrecked vehicles, prosecutors said. Colin Jackson, 39, was found guilty Wednesday following a seven-day trial before U.S. District Judge Trina L. Thompson, according to the U.S. Attorney’s Office. Prosecutors said Jackson conspired with others, including previously convicted defendant Kirill Afanasyev, to defraud an automobile insurance company by filing false claims involving damaged cars that had already been wrecked. According to prosecutors, evidence presented at trial showed Jackson obtained an insurance policy in 2018 on a vehicle that was undrivable and made false statements on the application, including the car’s estimated annual mileage. Months later, Jackson and Afanasyev submitted a fraudulent claim involving a staged accident, prosecutors said. The insurance company, unaware the vehicle had already been wrecked, approved the claim and paid Jackson about $27,000, prosecutors said. Authorities said the pair carried out a similar scheme in 2017 involving another damaged vehicle, resulting in an insurance payout of about $30,000. Jackson is scheduled to be sentenced Sept. 25. Federal officials said more than a dozen defendants have pleaded guilty or been convicted as part of an ongoing investigation into automobile insurance fraud and a related arson conspiracy.

headlines.title: Golden State Valkyries kick off regular season with a win and high hopes
headlines.description: The Golden State Valkyries played the first regular-season game of their second season on Friday night. The team played in Seattle, where they were able to lock in a win over the Seattle Storm. The Valkyries hosted a watch party for Bay Area fans at the Thrive City Plaza outside Chase Center in San Francisco. Several hundred people showed up to watch the game on the big screen, including many season-ticket holders. As the team starts its second season, fans say their historic first season built up a loyal fanbase. “I see a lot more purple around, and it’s always really exciting to see someone else wearing Valkyries [gear] here cause I just kind of point at them — and that camaraderie of a whole new fanbase to geek out with,” said season ticketholder Mace Mamlock of San Francisco. The Valkyries sold out game after game and became the first WNBA expansion team to make it to the playoffs in their first season. A recent CNBC ranking of team valuations showed that the team is now worth one billion dollars, which is a WNBA record. The team’s impact is also being felt by businesses around Chase Center. Parisha Elviano with Fikscue Barbecue at Thrive City said of the Valkyries that businesses feel “really blessed to have them beginning their second season here, and a lot of crowds coming in.” The team is also leaning into Bay Area basketball history. On Friday, they unveiled their new ‘Rebel’ jerseys which pay homage to past women’s teams like the San Francisco Pioneers and the San Jose Lasers. The Valkyries will play their home opener game on Sunday evening, and businesses nearby said they expect the game to draw big crowds to Chase Center.

headlines.title: Candidates for California governor talk about housing crisis at forum in Oakland
headlines.description: Five candidates running to become California’s next governor were in Oakland on Friday to talk about one of the biggest issues facing Bay Area voters: Housing. The forum was hosted at the Henry J. Kaiser Center and focused on the state’s housing crisis. NBC Bay Area was able to speak to four of the candidates about why this issue is central to their campaigns. Those candidates were Matt Mahan, Xavier Becerra, Katie Porter and Tom Steyer. The fifth candidate decided not to talk. The two leading Republican candidates for governor were also invited but decided not to attend. The discussion comes less than a month before the June primary, with mail-in voting already underway and many Bay Area voters feeling priced out. All four candidates in attendance agreed California has a housing affordability crisis. Pete Suratos has the full report in the video player above.

headlines.title: Contra Costa County anti-sprawl measure sparks debate
headlines.description: People in Contra Costa County will be voting on a measure that’s pitting conservationists against housing advocates. The county has had a voter-approved boundary protecting open space for decades. Voters will decide whether to extend it for another 25 years. Real Estate Attorney and San Ramon resident Vince Moita says the county is dealing with a housing affordability crisis. He says an urban sprawl measure on the ballot, Measure A, will only make it worse. “When you look at Contra Costa County 65% of the land will be restricted for at least the next 25 years to be non-urban,” Moita said. Measure A extends a decades old policy that would protect 65% of Contra Costa County’s land from development until at least 2051, leaving in place an urban limit line. “If we didn’t have this line, it would make it a lot easier for developers to build in areas that are the worst suited for development,” said Juan Pablo Galvan Martinez with Save Mt Diablo. Galvan Martinez says it’s critical to protect the county’s open space that’s home to countless wildlife species. “You have an incredible number of species of wildlife plant and other sorts of organisms that not only live here. Many of which are found nowhere else on earth,” he said. Galvan Martinez says the urban limit line has worked well for the last 36 years at preventing urban sprawl that creates traffic and keeping developers from building in high fire zones. “Most of the county outside the urban limit line is very high or high fire hazard zones meaning they’re the most likely to burn and the least suitable for development,” he said. Moita says it’s time to do away with the urban limit line and make way for growth. It’ll be up to voters to decide on June 2.

headlines.title: 1994 U.S. Men's National Soccer Team honored at America SCORES gala in San Jose
headlines.description: The soccer community came together Friday to honor the iconic 1994 U.S. Men’s National Soccer Team during the America SCORES Soccer Legacy Gala in downtown San Jose. The event, presented by Coca-Cola and Airbnb, started at 5 p.m. at the Signia by Hilton. The event honored the “iconic legacy” of the 1994 U.S. Men’s National Team, whose “groundbreaking performance ignited modern American soccer and helped shape its future,” according to organizers. Organizers said America SCORES was born thanks to that legacy, and began transforming young lives through soccer, poetry and service. Organizers added the gathering marks the 25th anniversary of the Bay Area out-of-school program.

headlines.title: Low clouds expected to cause flight delays at SFO
headlines.description: A ground delay program released by the Federal Aviation Administration on Friday morning is expected to cause major delays for hundreds of flights at San Francisco International Airport throughout the day. A low cloud ceiling at SFO prompted the FAA to delay flights departing and arriving from the airport between 8 a.m. and 11 p.m. Friday, with disruptions expected to average 70 minutes. The program will affect all flights flying to and from San Francisco in the contiguous United States and select Canadian cities, with minimal impact expected on arriving international flights. Flights destined for SFO will delay their departures, especially those within California, to comply with the policy of only 36 arrivals an hour. Decreased visibility due to low clouds requires more aircraft to have more separation when in the air, to ensure the safety of each airplane. Two of the airport’s runways are undergoing maintenance, limiting the available runways for takeoffs and landings. An airport spokesperson said over 200 flights were already experiencing delays as of 11 a.m., while more are expected throughout the day. Flight tracking website FlightAware reported the average delay at SFO was an hour and 15 minutes. United, Southwest, and SkyWest were the airlines with the most delays reported so far. Oakland San Francisco Bay Airport and San Jose Mineta International Airport are each experiencing 22 delays, according to FlightAware. Neither airport, however, has received a ground delay program advisory from the FAA. Travelers are advised to check with their respective airlines to see how their itineraries may have been impacted.
headlines.source: Andres Jimenez Larios | Bay City News

headlines.title: Brisbane police help rescue young sea lion spotted on roadway
headlines.description: Police helped rescue a young sea lion spotted on the roadway in Brisbane on Friday. Authorities said an off-duty Brisbane PD officer was on his way to work when they spotted the sea lion crossing the roadway by the DoubleTree Hotel, heading toward the Highway 101 ramp. Concerned about the safety of the sea lion and the motorists, the officer used his personal vehicle to slow down traffic and called the police department for assistance, according to officials. Police said officers secured the area and worked to safely contain the seal while waiting for personnel from the Marine Mammal Center. Personnel arrived at the scene and transported the seal to their veterinary hospital to receive treatment and rehabilitation with the goal of being released back into its natural environment. The Marine Mammal Center told officers they had been trying to locate the seal for the past several days after sightings near Coyote Point and surrounding shoreline areas. Authorities add the seal was determined to be malnourished and needed urgent care. Additionally, the police department reminds the public that if they encounter marine wildlife in distress, do not attempt to handle, feed, move or approach the animal themselves; instead, maintain a safe distance of at least 50 yards and contact trained professionals at the Marine Mamman Center at 415-289-7325.

headlines.title: Bay Area kitten season in full bloom
headlines.description: A corner of a Brentwood Petco store was squirming as furry kittens yelped, sucked milk from bottles, languished in cages and took in the team of the newly formed Ohana Paw Patrol Rescue — a group aimed at finding homes for orphaned kittens in the area. “We help with rescuing cats as in they bring kittens to us,” said the group’s founder Annetta Giambruno-Mobley. “We help to get them healthy and get them ready — they’re big enough to get shots and get fixed.” The group gathered to draw attention to what some call “kitten season,” the period between March and October when cats are most likely to have litters. Stray cat populations have exploded around the state, especially in the Central Valley and Bay Area, straining the resources of cities and organizations like the SPCA to care for them. In the past couple weeks, the Ohana group alone has taken in two dozen litters. “There’s way too many cats out there that aren’t being fixed,” Giambruno-Mobley said. “People are leaving them when they move away. People are getting kittens for free and think free is forever.” Ohana’s group of 14 volunteer foster families take in the kittens, some just days and weeks old, and nurse them to health. Volunteers like Janine Casey-Hammond rise every two hours at night to feed the kittens a bottle — their eyes still closed and unable to even go to the bathroom without help. “If you think about it, they’re a lot like other kinds of babies where they’re got to bed feed around the clock,” said Casey-Hammond. “Their mom would do that if we had their mom, which we don’t have their mom.” Volunteer foster Janine Casey-Hammond of Ohana Paw Patrol rescue bottle feeds a kitten. The Ohana Paw Patrol provides the fosters with food, litter, bedding and medical supplies. The volunteers provide the love, time and care. “I feel really accomplished helping all these cats that come from the streets or bad homes and re-homing them to forever families that will treat them right,” said foster volunteer Lily Quiles. The East Bay SPCA has experienced firsthand the cat-astrophe of unchecked feral cat litters. Last year, the organization took in as many as 1,600 kittens during kitten season. Eight hundred went to foster families. Joseph Romero of the East Bay SPCA said this year’s kitten season got underway early. “We will get in neonates almost on a daily basis, so just trying to find fosters to care for all those animals,” Romero said. “We can’t leave them in the shelter over night because they can’t take care of themselves.” The SPCA also uses volunteer foster families to take in the cats until they can get fixed and adopted. Romero said it’s common for people to spot unattended kittens in the wild and bring them into the shelter. But most often, the mother is simply off gathering food, which takes her away from the litter for hours. “They sort of kitten-nap them,” Romero said. “They do it with the best of intentions.” Volunteer foster Janine Casey-Hammond of Ohana Paw Patrol rescue bottle feeds a kitten. Romero recommended people leave litters of abandoned kittens alone, unless they go more than a day with no mother in sight. Giambruno-Mobley said the goal of saving the kittens and getting them fixed is tricky, as many cities have varying policies. Some cities are no-kill, some will resort to felicide. “We’re trying to save them,” Giambruno-Mobley said. “They don’t care how they’re getting rid of them so that makes it kind of harsh in some cities.” But Giambruno-Mobley said the unchecked populations of feral cats is a major issue, one her group is hoping to address by getting as many kittens fixed and adopted as possible. “We will always answer the phone,” Giambruno-Mobley said. “And if we can’t take their kittens in, we help them come up with a solution so they’re not floundering out there.”

headlines.title: Google settles racial discrimination lawsuit for $50 million
headlines.description: Google has settled with Black employees who alleged systemic racial disparities in hiring, pay, and advancement in a lawsuit filed in 2022. April Curley, a former Google employee, had sued the tech giant for racial discrimination, saying it engages in a “pattern and practice” of unfair treatment for its Black workers. The suit claimed the company steered them into lower-level and lower-paid jobs and subjected them to a hostile work environment if they speak out. Other former Google workers also joined the suit, which later received class action status. “This case is about accountability, plain and simple,” said civil rights attorney Ben Crump, who represented the plaintiffs, in a statement. “For far too long, Black employees in the tech industry have faced barriers that limit opportunity. This settlement is a significant step toward holding one of the world’s most powerful companies accountable and making clear that discriminatory practices cannot and will not be tolerated.” The settlement was announced in May 2025 and granted final approval this week. Google said when the settlement was reached that it strongly disagrees with the allegations that it treated anyone improperly and remains “committed to paying, hiring, and leveling all employees consistently.” The lawsuit, echoed years of complaints from Black employees at the company. That includes prominent artificial intelligence scholar Timnit Gebru, who said she was pushed out in 2020 after a dispute over a research paper examining the societal dangers of an emerging branch of artificial intelligence. The 2022 lawsuit claimed that Mountain View, California-based Google viewed Black job candidates “through harmful racial stereotypes” and claimed that hiring managers deemed Black candidates “not ‘Googly’ enough, a plain dog whistle for race discrimination.” In addition, according to the suit, interviewers “hazed” and undermined Black candidates and hired Black candidates into lower-paying and lower-level roles with less advancement potential based on their race and racial stereotypes. The settlement, which does not constitute admission of liability by Google, also includes a commitment to pay equity analyses, pay transparency measures, and limits on mandatory arbitration for employment-related disputes through at least August 2026, according to Crump.

headlines.title: Canvas system is online after cyberattack disrupted thousands of schools
headlines.description: Tens of thousands of students studying for final exams around the world Friday regained access to a key online learning system after a cyberattack knocked it offline, throwing schools and universities into chaos. A hacking group called ShinyHunters claimed responsibility for the breach at Canvas, said Luke Connolly, a threat analyst at the cybersecurity firm Emsisoft. Instructure, the company behind Canvas, said in an update late Thursday that the system was available for most users. Canvas is used to manage grades, course notes, assignments, lecture videos and more. The hacking group posted online that nearly 9,000 schools worldwide were affected, with billions of private messages and other records accessed, Connolly said. Screenshots Connolly provided showed that the group had been threatening to leak the trove of data. By Friday, Instructure and Canvas had been removed from a dedicated leak site created by the ransomware group on the dark web to publish stolen data, he said. Canvas went down Thursday at the worst possible time, which came as no surprise to Huseyin Can Yuceel, the security research lead at Picus Labs. “Timing is everything, because they want to inflict pain as much as possible,” he said, “so they can extort money out of it.” Colleges & Universities May 7 Education May 7 Rich in digitized data, the nation’s schools are prime targets for far-flung criminal hackers, who are assiduously locating and scooping up sensitive files that not long ago were committed to paper in locked cabinets. Past attacks have hit Minneapolis Public Schools and the Los Angeles Unified School District. Instructure has not posted about the attack on its social media. The company didn’t immediately respond to emails from The Associated Press asking whether it paid a ransom and inquiring about what happened with the compromised data. Students quickly took to social media, with many panicking that they could no longer view course materials housed within the platform to study for their final exams. Teachers said they were having to find workarounds to help students study for exams and submit final assignments. And some schools, such as the University of Texas at San Antonio, announced they were pushing back finals scheduled for Friday in response to the outage. Schools like Princeton University turned to the social platform X late Thursday to announce that Canvas appeared to be working and information technology staff was monitoring the situation. Rhongho Jang, a computer science professor at Wayne State University in Detroit, was finalizing grades for a class of 94 students when the system went down. He keeps paper copies of the student exams, but all of the semester assignments were done online. “That’s 50% weight for the final grading,” Jang said. If those assignments and grades could not be recovered, Jang would have given his students full credit. “I didn’t want to penalize them,” he said. “We cannot judge based on the data we don’t have. The final responsibility is still on the server.” Although he was sure the data was saved and would be recovered, Jang was relieved when Canvas came back up. Allan Liska, of the cybersecurity firm Recorded Future, said the outage did appear deliberate, not a glitch, and that Instructure was trying to figure out how widespread the problem was and make sure the hackers were no longer inside its system. “There’s no indication at this point that any ransom has been paid,” Liska said. “And it likely is still a little too early for a ransom to have been paid. You know, normally these negotiations kind of drag on for a while.” Liska said nothing big has been leaked yet, but said that is common. “Once they’ve leaked, they’ve lost their leverage.” Connolly said the Canvas attack is strikingly similar to a breach at PowerSchool, which also offers learning management tools. In that case, a Massachusetts college student was charged. Connolly described ShinyHunters as a loose affiliation of teenagers and young adults based in the U.S. and the United Kingdom. The group also has been tied to other attacks, including Live Nation’s Ticketmaster subsidiary. ShinyHunters, or an offshoot, also was behind a previous smaller breach of Instructure, Liska said. He added that the group or someone pretending to be ShinyHunters issued a statement Friday indicating that it had nothing to say. “It’s very weird,” Liska said, noting that the group is “normally a very talkative bunch.”
